questions.question: In Angular's decorator component API, how does data flow between a parent and child component?
options: Parent to child via `@Input()`; child to parent via `@Output()` events
Child to parent via `@Input()`; parent to child via `@Output()` events
Both directions via `@Input()`; `@Output()` is only for sibling communication
The child directly mutates the parent's properties through a shared reference
questions.explanation: Data flow is one-way: parent to child through inputs, child to parent through output events. A component never reaches up to mutate its parent; it emits and the parent decides what to do.

questions.question: What does `@Input({ required: true })` do when the parent omits the binding?
options: Angular errors at build time
Angular silently assigns `null` at runtime
Angular logs a console warning but renders normally
Angular waits and throws only after `ngAfterViewInit`
questions.explanation: `{ required: true }` (added v16) makes Angular error at build time if the parent omits the binding, so the field doesn't need to be nullable. It is a compile-time guarantee, not a runtime warning.

questions.question: Which built-in transforms coerce attribute strings, so that `@Input({ transform: booleanAttribute })` turns `disabled=""` into a real boolean?
options: `booleanAttribute` and `numberAttribute`
`coerceBoolean` and `coerceNumber`
`parseBool` and `parseInt`
`asBoolean` and `asNumber`
questions.explanation: Angular ships `booleanAttribute` and `numberAttribute` (added v16.1) to handle attribute-string coercion in the input `transform` option. The other names do not exist in `@angular/core`.

questions.question: Two-way binding `[(x)]` is sugar for which pair of one-way bindings?
options: `[x]` input plus `(xChange)` output
`[x]` input plus `(x)` output
`(x)` output plus `[xValue]` input
`[x]` input plus `(xUpdate)` output
questions.explanation: `[(x)]` expands to `[x]="..."` plus `(xChange)="..."`: an input `x` paired with an output named exactly `xChange`. This `x`/`xChange` convention is what `model()` collapses into one declaration.

questions.question: Which lifecycle hook is the first safe point to read a `@ViewChild` query result?
options: `ngAfterViewInit`
`ngOnInit`
the constructor
`ngAfterContentInit`
questions.explanation: Decorator view query results land in `ngAfterViewInit`. Reading them in `ngOnInit` or the constructor gives `undefined` (unless `static: true`); `ngAfterContentInit` is for content queries, not view queries.

questions.question: Given this component, what does calling `this.count.set(5)` do?
options: Updates the signal AND emits the auto-created `countChange` output
Updates the signal only; the parent must manually re-read it
Emits `countChange` only; the local signal value stays unchanged
Throws, because a `model()` signal is read-only and cannot be set
questions.explanation: `model()` is a writable two-way signal that auto-creates a matching `countChange` output. Writing it with `set`/`update` both updates the signal locally and emits the change event so a parent's `[(count)]` stays in sync.

questions.question: What type does the signals-first `output()` function return, and what is it?
options: An `OutputEmitterRef<T>` — a purpose-built emitter, not an `EventEmitter` or Observable
An `EventEmitter<T>` — the same RxJS-backed class as the decorator API
An `InputSignal<T>` — a read-only signal you call to get the last value
An `Observable<T>` you subscribe to directly with `.pipe()`
questions.explanation: `output()` returns an `OutputEmitterRef<T>`, a purpose-built emitter with `emit()` and a `subscribe()` (via `OutputRef`); it is explicitly NOT an `EventEmitter` and NOT an Observable. Only `input()`/`model()` produce signals.

questions.question: What is the resolved TypeScript type of a field declared `id = input.required<string>()`?
options: `InputSignal<string>` — `undefined` is dropped from the type
`InputSignal<string | undefined>` — required is only enforced at runtime
`string` — it is a plain property, so calling it is unnecessary
`Signal<string | null>` — required inputs default to `null`
questions.explanation: `input.required<T>()` produces an `InputSignal<T>` whose read type is exactly `T` with `undefined` dropped, because the build fails if the parent omits the binding. This is a key advantage over the decorator's `{ required: true }`, which still leaves a nullable type.

questions.question: Content projected through `<ng-content>` is created and change-detected in the parent's context, so even an `OnPush` child cannot skip checking it.
questions.explanation: Projected content is created and change-detected in the parent's context, not the child's. Because of this, an `OnPush` child cannot skip checking projected content even when its own inputs are unchanged.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `ngProjectAs`, and what is its key restriction?
options: It makes an element match a different projection selector than its own identity; it accepts only a static value
It renames the slot inside the child component; it accepts any bound expression
It forces projected content into the child's change-detection context; it must be a signal
It duplicates projected content across multiple slots; it takes a comma-separated list
questions.explanation: `ngProjectAs` makes Angular match an element (often an `ng-container` wrapper) against a different selector than its own identity. It accepts only a static value, never a bound expression.

questions.question: A signal input drives derived state. Why does the modern API rarely need `ngOnChanges` to react to that input changing?
options: A `computed` re-runs automatically whenever any input signal it reads changes
`ngOnChanges` is deprecated and removed in v19, so it cannot be used
Signal inputs trigger `ngOnChanges` only on the first change, not after
`computed` caches forever and never recomputes, so no hook is needed
questions.explanation: An input is a signal dependency; a `computed` recomputes automatically when any signal it reads changes, so `ngOnChanges` is rarely needed. `ngOnChanges` is not removed, and `computed` recomputes lazily on dependency change rather than caching forever.

questions.question: Which statements about `@ViewChild(X, { static: true })` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It resolves the query before the first change detection, so the result is readable in `ngOnInit`
It is only valid when the target isn't inside `@if`/`@for`
A static result does not update afterward if the matched node changes
It makes the query result reactive, updating whenever the matched node changes
Signal queries expose the same `static` flag for parity
questions.explanation: `static: true` resolves the query before the first change detection (readable in `ngOnInit`), is only valid when the target isn't inside structural control flow, and the result does not update afterward. Signal queries have no `static` flag and are always reactive.

questions.question: Piping an Observable through `takeUntilDestroyed()` in an injection context causes it to auto-complete when the component is destroyed, removing the need for a manual `ngOnDestroy` Subject teardown.
questions.explanation: `takeUntilDestroyed()` (called in an injection context) hooks into `DestroyRef` so the stream auto-completes on destroy, eliminating the classic manual `ngOnDestroy` + `Subject` unsubscribe dance.

questions.question: A parent wraps projected content in `@if(show)`. When `show` becomes `false` at runtime, what happens to the slot's fallback content (v18+)?
options: The fallback does NOT re-appear — slot assignment is decided once at creation
The fallback re-appears immediately because the slot is now empty
Angular throws, because `<ng-content>` cannot be placed near `@if`
The previously projected content stays rendered until the next CD cycle
questions.explanation: `<ng-content>` slotting is static: slot assignment is decided once at component creation. If projected content is supplied conditionally and the condition later turns false, the fallback does not re-appear, which is a documented gotcha.

questions.question: In v20, what happened to the former `afterRender` standalone function?
options: It was renamed to `afterEveryRender` and the old name was dropped with no alias
It was renamed to `afterEveryRender` but the old `afterRender` alias still works
It was merged into `afterNextRender`, which now takes a `repeat: true` option
It was deprecated in favor of the `ngAfterRender` lifecycle interface
questions.explanation: `afterEveryRender` is the v20 rename of the former `afterRender` (now stable), and the old name was dropped without a compatibility alias. It remains a browser-only standalone function, not a lifecycle interface method.

questions.question: Why is reading a decorator `@Input()` value inside the constructor a bug?
options: Inputs are not yet set in the constructor; input-dependent setup belongs in `ngOnInit`
The constructor runs after `ngOnInit`, so the value is already stale
Constructors cannot access `this`, so the property is unreadable
Inputs are write-only until `ngAfterViewInit` resolves them
questions.explanation: The constructor runs first and is for DI/field initialization only; inputs are not yet bound, so the value is undefined there. Input-dependent setup goes in `ngOnInit`, which runs once after the first `ngOnChanges` when inputs are set.

questions.question: Considering the standard Angular lifecycle order, which statements are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)
options: `ngOnChanges` fires before `ngOnInit` on the first cycle and again on every subsequent decorator-input change
`ngAfterContentInit` fires before `ngAfterViewInit`
`afterNextRender` / `afterEveryRender` callbacks run only in the browser and are skipped during SSR/prerender
`ngDoCheck` runs exactly once, right after `ngOnInit`
`ngOnDestroy` fires before `ngAfterViewChecked` on the first cycle
questions.explanation: `ngOnChanges` precedes the first `ngOnInit` and re-fires on each decorator-input change; content hooks precede view hooks; render callbacks are browser-only. `ngDoCheck` fires on every change-detection run (not once), and `ngOnDestroy` runs at teardown, after the init/checked hooks.

questions.question: Which statements about the signals-first component I/O and query APIs are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: An `input()` returns a read-only `InputSignal<T>`; you cannot write to it from inside the component
Signal queries compose in `computed`/`effect`, whereas decorator queries do not
`viewChildren()` and `contentChildren()` have no `.required` variant
`output()` produces a signal you can read with `()` to get the last emitted value
Signal queries support a `static` flag just like decorator queries
questions.explanation: `input()` is read-only; signal queries (unlike decorator queries) feed `computed`/`effect`; and the multi-element `viewChildren`/`contentChildren` have no `.required` variant. Outputs are not signals, and signal queries have no `static` flag because they are always reactive.
